evidence_quotes.quote: the practice of publishers destroying or rendering unplayable digital video games after their purchase.

paragraphs.text: To the esteemed members of the European Commission, I am writing to provide feedback on the 2024 fitness check on digital fairness. I strongly support the goals of this initiative to ensure fair practices and consumer protection in the online realm. In particular, I wish to highlight a significant area where stronger consumer protections are urgently needed: the practice of publishers destroying or rendering unplayable digital video games after their purchase. As a dedicated consumer of digital entertainment, I have personally experienced and observed the growing trend of publishers removing games from digital storefronts, shutting down essential servers, or altering core functionalities post-purchase. This leads to a situation where a product, often purchased with the expectation of long-term access and enjoyment, becomes inaccessible or significantly degraded through no fault of the consumer. This practice feels inherently unfair and, in my view, directly contradicts the spirit of consumer protection that the Digital Fairness Act aims to uphold. Consider the parallels with other digital products or even physical goods. If I purchase a book, a film, or a piece of software, I expect to retain access to it. The idea that a publisher could unilaterally decide to make a purchased item unusable after the point of sale is deeply concerning and eroding consumer trust in digital markets. It can lead to significant financial loss for consumers and fosters a sense of insecurity about digital ownership. I respectfully request that the Commission consider incorporating provisions within the Digital Fairness Act, or future related legislation, to protect consumers from these destructive practices. Specifically, measures could include: - Mandating continued access to purchased digital games: Even if a game is no longer sold, consumers who have purchased it should retain the right to download and play it, ideally with offline functionality or through publicly available server emulation where online features are essential. - Preventing the removal of essential functionalities: Publishers should be prohibited from removing features or access necessary for the core enjoyment of a game after its sale, without providing a viable and fair alternative. - Establishing clear policies for end-of-life digital products: There should be regulations that ensure transparency and fair compensation or alternative solutions for consumers when a digital product is genuinely reaching its end-of-life cycle. Protecting consumers from unfair commercial practices related to "dark patterns" and "misleading marketing" is crucial, and I believe that the unilateral destruction of purchased digital content by publishers constitutes an equally significant, albeit different, form of unfair practice. Addressing this issue would significantly enhance consumer confidence and truly embody the principles of digital fairness. Thank you for your time and consideration of this critical matter.
